High-Performance Computing on Lonestar6 Rich Herrington DRI, Research Computing Services Chapter 1 High-Performance Computing on Lonestar6 1.1 Overview This book is a practical introduction to high-performance computing using the Lonestar6 (LS6) system at the Texas Advanced Computing Center (TACC). Use it as: your course text your guided lab manual your workflow reference when you need to remember how to do something correctly on the cluster The goal is not only to help you run commands. The goal is to help you build good HPC habits: inspecting the environment, organizing files, requesting resources responsibly, interpreting output, and debugging from evidence rather than guesswork. Across the book you will move from: first login and basic HPC ideas to Linux fundamentals to SLURM job scheduling to compiling software to parallel computing to containers and reproducible workflows to a capstone project that brings the workflow together 1.2 What You Should Learn from This Book By the end of the book, you should be able to: explain the difference between login nodes and compute nodes navigate Linux confidently on a shared system manage files, permissions, and environment variables use modules to access software and compilers write, submit, monitor, and debug SLURM jobs compile and run C and C++ programs on an HPC system distinguish between serial, threaded, and distributed execution explain the roles of MPI, OpenMP, and containers in HPC workflows document an HPC workflow clearly enough that another user can reproduce it 1.3 What This Book Emphasizes Each chapter returns to the same core questions: What is this concept or tool for? What mental model should you use to understand it? What commands do you actually run? What usually goes wrong? How do you debug it when it fails? HPC rewards careful, inspectable, repeatable work. If you can run one successful job but cannot explain the environment, correct a resource request, or diagnose a failed run, you are not yet working independently. This book is meant to help you cross that gap. 1.4 How to Use the Chapters Most chapters include some or all of the following: overview mental model worked examples common mistakes lab exercises stretch goals summary points The repetition is deliberate. Strong HPC work usually follows a pattern: inspect the system make or edit a file run a small test scale up through the scheduler inspect output revise and rerun if needed This pattern matters because HPC work is rarely one big leap. It is usually a series of small, checkable steps. 1.5 How to Study from This Book A good way to use the book is: read the overview first pay attention to the mental model type and run the examples yourself complete the lab at the end of the chapter keep the appendix nearby as a quick reference return to earlier chapters when something fails later Do not treat the book as something to read once and set aside. Use it while you work. 1.6 Working Assumptions You do not need prior experience with: cluster computing batch scheduling software compilation parallel programming containers You do need patience, attention to detail, and a willingness to test things incrementally. On HPC systems, small mistakes are normal. What matters is learning how to find them and correct them. 1.7 A Practical Rule for the Whole Book Keep this rule visible as you work: Do setup on the login node. Do serious computation on compute nodes through the scheduler. That one distinction will help you avoid many of the most common beginner mistakes. 1.8 Looking Ahead The first chapter begins with the most basic question: what makes an HPC system different from an ordinary computer, and how should you orient yourself inside that environment?

Chapter 2 Introduction to High-Performance Computing on LS6 2.1 Overview High-performance computing, or HPC, is the practice of using powerful shared computing systems to solve problems that are too large, too slow, or too resource-intensive for an ordinary laptop or desktop computer. In this book, we use Lonestar6 (LS6) as the working example. The goal is not just to define HPC, but to help you begin using it productively. By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: describe what a cluster is explain the difference between login nodes and compute nodes identify common LS6 storage locations connect the basic HPC workflow to the commands you will use later 2.2 Why people use HPC There are three common reasons people move from a local machine to an HPC system. 2.2.1 1. The job is too slow on one machine A simulation that would take hours or days on a laptop may complete much faster on multiple cores or multiple nodes. 2.2.2 2. The job needs more memory Some workflows fail locally not because the CPU is too slow, but because the dataset, model, or matrix is too large for the memory available on the machine. 2.2.3 3. The work can be split into parts Many scientific and data-intensive problems can be broken into pieces and executed in parallel. HPC systems are designed for this style of work. 2.3 Mental model: a cluster is a collection of computers An HPC system is not one giant computer. It is better to think of it as a coordinated collection of computers working together. Important terms: node: one computer within the cluster core: one CPU execution unit on a node memory (RAM): memory available to processes or threads running on a node interconnect: the high-speed network that links nodes together This distinction matters because later chapters will ask you to request resources in terms of nodes, tasks, and cores. 2.4 The most important distinction: login node vs compute node Beginners often assume that once they log in to the cluster, they are already in the right place to run their real workload. That is not usually true. 2.4.1 Login nodes Login nodes are used for: editing files navigating directories loading modules compiling small programs moving data submitting jobs Login nodes are not the place for serious computation. 2.4.2 Compute nodes Compute nodes are used for: running scheduled jobs interactive compute sessions parallel programs training or simulation workloads large production tasks 2.4.3 Rule of thumb Do setup on the login node. Do computation on compute nodes. That single idea prevents many of the worst beginner mistakes. 2.5 A simple cluster workflow Here is the workflow you will repeat throughout the course: Laptop | | ssh v Login node | edit files, inspect directories, load modules | write job scripts | | sbatch v SLURM scheduler | v Compute node(s) | v Output files and results The scheduler in this workflow is SLURM, which will become the center of Chapters 3 and 4. 2.6 Connecting to LS6 A standard SSH connection looks like this: ssh your_username@ls6.tacc.utexas.edu After login, you may see a shell prompt like: [username@login1 ~]$ This prompt tells you several things at once: your username the machine name your current directory The prompt is not decoration. It gives you context. 2.7 Storage areas you will encounter Cluster workflows usually involve more than one storage location. 2.7.1 $HOME Your home directory is a personal, relatively safe place for configuration files, small scripts, and lightweight persistent material. 2.7.2 $WORK This is often a project-oriented area where you keep larger files, datasets, software builds, and materials that need to persist longer than temporary scratch storage. 2.7.3 $SCRATCH This is usually a high-performance temporary storage area. It is useful for active computation and temporary job data, but it should not be treated as permanent archival storage. 2.8 Why directories matter so much in HPC On a laptop, users often rely on clicking around to find files. In an HPC workflow, your current directory strongly influences: where your job looks for input where output files are written whether commands run on the files you intended That is why pwd and ls show up constantly in beginner workflows. 2.9 Minimal Linux toolkit for starting HPC work You do not need to master Linux before beginning. You do need a small set of reliable commands. pwd ls cd mkdir cp mv rm nano chmod +x These commands are enough to support the first real workflow in the course. 2.10 First exercise: create a workspace mkdir hpc_course cd hpc_course pwd ls This does four useful things: creates a directory for your course work moves you into that directory verifies where you are confirms what is inside it A surprising number of job failures come from skipping these checks. 2.11 First shell script Create a file: nano hello.sh Add: echo "Hello from LS6" Make it executable: chmod +x hello.sh Run it: ./hello.sh 2.12 What just happened? Even this small example teaches several important HPC habits. You: created a script file made the file executable ran a lightweight command safely on a login node confirmed that your shell environment is working This is appropriate login-node work because the task is tiny and brief. 2.13 Common beginner mistakes in Chapter 1 2.13.1 Running heavy work on the login node This is the most common conceptual mistake. Users often think, “I am logged in, so I can start the analysis.” In HPC, logging in gives you access to the environment, not ownership of shared computational resources. 2.13.2 Losing track of the current directory If you do not know where you are, you do not really know what your job will act on. 2.13.3 Forgetting execute permission A file can exist and still fail to run. ls -l and chmod +x are basic troubleshooting tools. 2.13.4 Treating storage areas as interchangeable Different storage areas serve different purposes. That matters for both performance and data safety. 2.14 Chapter lab Log in to LS6. Create a directory named hpc_course. Move into it and verify with pwd. Create hello.sh. Add one echo command. Make the script executable. Run it. Create a second script that prints your username, current directory, and date. Suggested commands: whoami pwd date 2.15 Reflection questions Why is a login node not the right place for serious computation? What is the difference between logging in and actually running a job? Why does your current directory matter before job submission? Which storage location would you trust for temporary high-volume output, and which would you treat more carefully? 2.16 Looking ahead In the next chapter, we slow down and build the Linux and environment skills that make later SLURM work easier. The theme of Chapter 2 is simple: good HPC habits begin with filesystem awareness and environment awareness.

Chapter 3 Linux Fundamentals and Environment Awareness 3.1 Overview This chapter builds the Linux foundation needed for real HPC work. Many job failures that appear to be “cluster problems” are actually basic environment problems: wrong directory missing file missing execute permission wrong module environment command not found By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: interpret the output of ls -l change file permissions for common cases inspect and reason about environment variables understand what PATH does load, list, and unload modules use a practical debugging checklist before submitting work 3.2 Mental model: an HPC system is a shared environment On a personal laptop, users often assume that their preferred software, file layout, and editor are just “there.” On an HPC system, the environment is more explicit. You are working in a space that is: multi-user resource-managed environment-driven structured for repeatability That means your workflow should be deliberate. You should know: where you are what files are present what software is loaded what commands are available in your environment 3.3 Inspecting the filesystem The first two commands to trust are: pwd ls -l 3.3.1 pwd Prints the current working directory. 3.3.2 ls -l Lists files with useful details such as permissions, owner, group, size, and modification time. An example permission string looks like this: -rwxr-xr-- A good beginner interpretation is: the file exists it has an owner, group, and permission settings some users may execute it, some may not 3.4 File permissions in practice Permissions matter constantly in Linux-based HPC workflows. You do not need a full theory of Unix security to start working productively. You do need the operational basics. 3.4.1 Common actions Make a file executable: chmod +x script.sh Remove write permission: chmod -w file.txt Use numeric mode: chmod 755 script.sh A helpful mental model for 755 is: owner: read, write, execute group: read, execute others: read, execute 3.5 Why permissions matter on clusters Common failures include: a script exists but will not execute a file cannot be edited because it is not writable a shared file has the wrong permissions for collaborators When a script “should work” but does not, ls -l is one of the fastest checks you can make. 3.6 Environment variables Environment variables define part of the context in which commands run. View them with: env Two important examples are: echo $HOME echo $PATH 3.6.1 $HOME Usually points to your home directory. 3.6.2 $PATH Tells the shell where to search for executable programs. If a program is not on your PATH, typing its name may produce: command not found 3.7 A practical mental model for PATH Think of PATH as a list of directories the shell searches when you enter a command name. If gcc is on your PATH, you can type: gcc --version If not, the shell may not know where to find it. A temporary way to add your own directory is: export PATH=$HOME/bin:$PATH This prepends $HOME/bin to the front of the search path for the current shell session. 3.8 Modules: software is part of the environment Clusters often use a module system rather than making every software package globally visible at all times. That means software is often loaded into the environment when needed. Common commands: module avail module load gcc module list module unload gcc 3.9 Why modules matter On an HPC system, different projects may need: different compiler versions different MPI libraries different Python stacks different container runtimes Modules help manage this complexity. Instead of assuming “the system version” is correct, users load an appropriate environment for the job. 3.10 Example: loading a compiler module load gcc which gcc gcc --version This sequence is valuable because it does three things: loads the compiler module shows which executable will run shows the version now active in the shell 3.11 Practical file inspection tools A few file-related commands are worth using early. Find a file: find . -name "example.txt" View a file: cat example.txt less example.txt head example.txt tail example.txt These tools are especially helpful when checking logs, outputs, and small configuration files. 3.12 Piping commands together One of the most useful Linux habits is combining small commands. cat file.txt | grep "error" This is a classic pipeline: one command produces output, the next filters it. You do not need to become an expert in shell pipelines immediately, but you should recognize that Linux tools are designed to be chained together. 3.13 Disk awareness matters HPC users need at least basic awareness of storage usage. Check filesystem space: df -h Check directory sizes: du -sh * These commands help prevent confusion when jobs fail because of full storage areas or because output is larger than expected. 3.14 Process awareness Sometimes you need to inspect or stop your own processes. List your processes: ps -u $USER Kill a process: kill <pid> This becomes more useful later when dealing with editors, accidental runs, or interactive sessions. 3.15 Optional connection to R If you use R heavily, it is useful to remember that environment information can also be inspected from inside R. Sys.getenv("PATH") And shell commands can be invoked from R when appropriate: system("echo $HOME") That said, it is still important to understand the shell environment directly rather than relying only on IDE tools. 3.16 A debugging mindset for Linux-based HPC work When something goes wrong, start with evidence. Ask these five questions in order: 3.16.1 1. Where am I? pwd 3.16.2 2. What files are here? ls -l 3.16.3 3. Do I have the right permissions? ls -l script.sh 3.16.4 4. Is the program actually available? which gcc 3.16.5 5. Is my environment what I think it is? module list This checklist will reappear in later chapters because it prevents premature guessing. 3.17 Common beginner mistakes in Chapter 2 forgetting chmod +x assuming PATH is correct without checking forgetting to load a needed module filling up $HOME and wondering why workflows behave strangely treating missing commands as mysterious system failures instead of environment issues 3.18 Chapter lab 3.18.1 Part 1: permissions touch test.sh ls -l chmod +x test.sh ls -l 3.18.2 Part 2: environment variables echo $HOME echo $PATH export COURSE=HPC echo $COURSE 3.18.3 Part 3: modules module avail module load gcc module list which gcc gcc --version 3.18.4 Part 4: storage awareness df -h du -sh ~ 3.18.5 Part 5: file discovery touch example.txt find . -name "example.txt" 3.19 Stretch goal Create a custom bin/ directory in your home space, add it to PATH, place a small script in that directory, and run it without typing the full path. 3.20 Looking ahead The next chapter turns these Linux and environment skills into a real HPC workflow. Once you can reliably manage directories, permissions, and modules, you are ready to write a SLURM script and submit work to the scheduler.

Chapter 4 SLURM Job Scheduling on LS6 4.1 Overview On a shared HPC system, real computational work is normally submitted through a scheduler rather than run directly on the login node. On LS6, that scheduler is SLURM. That sentence is easy to memorize, but the course goal is deeper than memorization. You should understand why a scheduler exists, what a job request really means, and how to reason about a job when it does not behave the way you expected. This chapter develops the practical workflow for beginner and intermediate batch jobs: write a job script request resources deliberately submit with sbatch monitor with squeue inspect finished jobs with sacct interpret output files diagnose common failures By the end of the chapter, you should be able to submit a real job, explain the meaning of your resource request, and work through the first layer of job debugging without guessing. 4.2 Mental model: SLURM is a resource manager, not a launch button A common beginner misunderstanding is to treat sbatch as if it were a special version of bash. It is not. When you run a shell script directly, the shell starts executing the script immediately in your current environment. When you submit with sbatch, you are doing something different: you hand SLURM a script you describe the resources needed for that script the scheduler decides when those resources are available the script later runs on one or more compute nodes So a SLURM job is not just “commands to run.” It is a contract between you and the scheduler: Here is the work I want to do, here are the resources I believe it needs, and here is how long I think it should take. That is why a SLURM script is both: a shell script, and a resource request document 4.3 Why clusters use schedulers A scheduler exists because HPC systems are shared. Without a scheduler: users would compete chaotically for access large jobs could crowd out smaller ones there would be no orderly queue tracking usage would be difficult fairness would break down quickly With a scheduler: work is queued resource use is explicit policies can be enforced accounting is possible compute nodes stay organized as a shared service rather than a free-for-all This is one reason HPC feels different from a personal workstation. On your laptop, you usually ask, “Can my machine run this?” On a cluster, you also ask, “What is the appropriate request for a shared system?” 4.4 The basic batch workflow Write job script ↓ Submit with sbatch ↓ Job enters queue ↓ SLURM decides when resources are available ↓ Job starts on compute node(s) ↓ Output and accounting records become available That workflow shows up again and again in HPC practice. Many later topics—parallel computing, containers, compiled programs, Python workflows, R workflows—still pass through the same scheduler logic. 4.5 Login nodes versus compute nodes This distinction remains central. 4.5.1 Login nodes are for: editing files reading documentation small test commands moving data compiling small programs submitting jobs 4.5.2 Compute nodes are for: real program execution long-running analyses memory-intensive work parallel workloads training or simulation runs A useful rule of thumb is: Use the login node to prepare work. Use compute nodes to perform work. If a task would monopolize CPU time, consume large memory, run for a long time, or scale across multiple cores, it belongs under SLURM. 4.6 Your first SLURM script Create a file: nano job.slurm Add: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J hello_job #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:01:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Hello from compute node" hostname date pwd This is intentionally simple. The goal is to make the scheduler behavior visible. 4.7 Reading the script line by line 4.7.1 Shebang #!/bin/bash Tells the system to execute the script using Bash. 4.7.2 Job name #SBATCH -J hello_job Gives your job a readable name. Good names matter because later you may have many jobs in the queue. 4.7.3 Nodes #SBATCH -N 1 Requests one node. 4.7.4 Tasks #SBATCH -n 1 Requests one task. At a beginner level, you can loosely think of a task as one program instance or one process slot. Later, for MPI jobs, the meaning becomes especially important. 4.7.5 Wall time #SBATCH -t 00:01:00 Tells SLURM the job should need at most one minute. This is not a prediction made for curiosity. It is part of scheduling. If you ask for far more time than you need, the job may wait longer than necessary. If you ask for too little time, the job may be terminated before finishing. 4.7.6 Partition #SBATCH -p normal Requests the normal partition. A partition is a queue or class of resources. Different systems define partitions differently, but the main mental model is simple: A partition tells SLURM which pool of resources and policies your job should be associated with. 4.8 Submit the job sbatch job.slurm Typical response: Submitted batch job 123456 That message does not mean your program has already run. It means SLURM has accepted the job into the scheduling system. This is the first beginner correction that really matters: sbatch submits. It does not promise immediate execution. 4.9 Check the queue Use: squeue -u $USER Typical fields include: job ID partition job name state elapsed time number of nodes If the job starts quickly, you may barely see it. If not, squeue will let you observe its status. 4.10 Common job states PD = pending R = running CG = completing CD = completed A pending job is not automatically a broken job. It may simply be waiting for resources, waiting behind other jobs, or waiting because your request is harder to place. 4.11 Output files are evidence When the batch job finishes, a file such as the following often appears: slurm-123456.out Inspect it with: cat slurm-123456.out or: less slurm-123456.out A strong beginner habit is: do not guess what happened inspect the output file Your output file is often the fastest route from confusion to evidence. 4.12 What happened in the first example? When hello_job runs successfully, you have completed a real HPC workflow: wrote a scheduler-aware script requested resources submitted the request waited for SLURM to place the job ran on a compute node captured output in a file That may seem small, but it is the foundation for almost everything else in the course. 4.13 Resource requests: what you are really asking for A job request is best understood as a statement of workload shape. Questions you should learn to ask: Is this a serial job or a parallel job? Does it need one core or many? Does it need one node or several nodes? Is it short or long? Does it require a special software environment? Is the request realistic or inflated? These questions matter because scheduler performance depends on matching the request to the workload. 4.13.1 Too small a request can cause: slow performance oversubscription thread/process mismatch job failure 4.13.2 Too large a request can cause: unnecessary queue delay wasted allocation poor cluster citizenship confusion when the code uses only a fraction of what was requested Responsible HPC practice is not “ask for everything.” It is “ask for what the workload actually needs.” 4.14 A better beginner script: make the environment visible A useful early script is one that prints context: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J context_job #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:02:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Job ID: $SLURM_JOB_ID" echo "Job name: $SLURM_JOB_NAME" echo "Running on host:" hostname echo "Working directory:" pwd echo "Date:" date This helps you see that jobs run in a real environment with real metadata. 4.15 The “sleep” demonstration To observe the queue and running state more clearly, use a job that lasts long enough to inspect. #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J sleep_demo #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:03:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Starting" hostname date sleep 30 echo "Finishing" date Submit it: sbatch sleep_demo.slurm Then watch: squeue -u $USER This is a small but powerful exercise because it makes the scheduler visible. 4.16 Why queue time can change You may wonder why one job starts immediately while another waits. The answer is usually some combination of: current system load partition policies how many resources were requested wall time requested job priority the shape of the available free resources This is a good place to build maturity. Queue time is not usually arbitrary. It is a consequence of your request meeting a real shared system. 4.17 Output and error handling By default, many simple jobs write output to a file such as: slurm-123456.out For more deliberate control, you can specify output files directly: #SBATCH -o job_%j.out #SBATCH -e job_%j.err where %j expands to the job ID. This helps separate standard output from standard error and makes debugging cleaner. Example: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J split_output #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:02:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o split_%j.out #SBATCH -e split_%j.err echo "Standard output message" ls does_not_exist This is a useful exercise because it helps you see that some messages belong in the output stream and others belong in the error stream. 4.18 A realistic serial job pattern Here is a more representative pattern for a serial program: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J serial_python #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:10:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o serial_%j.out #SBATCH -e serial_%j.err module load python python analysis.py This script has all the basic parts of real work: explicit resources explicit environment explicit program launch explicit output handling 4.19 A realistic compile-and-run pattern Sometimes the job script is used only to run an already-built executable: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J c_run #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o c_run_%j.out #SBATCH -e c_run_%j.err ./hello The separation matters: build small programs on the login node execute real workloads through SLURM 4.20 Common beginner mistakes 4.20.1 Mistake 1: Assuming sbatch ran the code right away Correction: sbatch submits to the scheduler. The code may run later. 4.20.2 Mistake 2: Running heavy work directly on the login node Correction: login nodes are for setup and lightweight tasks. 4.20.3 Mistake 3: Requesting many tasks for a serial program Correction: more requested resources do not help if the code uses only one process. 4.20.4 Mistake 4: Asking for far too much wall time Correction: inflated requests can delay scheduling and misrepresent workload needs. 4.20.5 Mistake 5: Ignoring output files Correction: output and error files are usually the first evidence to inspect. 4.20.6 Mistake 6: Submitting from the wrong directory Correction: confirm location with pwd and ls before submission. 4.21 A debugging checklist for failed jobs When a job behaves unexpectedly, ask the following in order. 4.21.1 1. Did the job submit? Use: squeue -u $USER If it is no longer there, it may have finished or failed quickly. 4.21.2 2. What does accounting say? Use: sacct or: sacct -j 123456 This helps confirm whether the job completed, failed, or was cancelled. 4.21.3 3. What do the output and error files say? Use: cat slurm-123456.out or your custom output/error filenames. 4.21.4 4. Was the intended program or file actually present? Use: pwd ls This checks your working directory assumptions. 4.21.5 5. Was the required software environment loaded? If your script assumes Python, GCC, MPI, or another toolchain, verify the appropriate module commands are present. 4.21.6 6. Did the resource request match the program model? A serial program with four tasks or an OpenMP program without thread settings may behave badly or misleadingly. 4.22 Failure case study 1: misspelled command Suppose the script contains: pythno analysis.py The job may submit successfully, but the output or error file will show that the command is not found. The key lesson is important: A job can fail even when submission succeeds. SLURM accepted the job request. The program inside the job then failed. 4.23 Failure case study 2: missing input file Suppose the script expects input.txt, but the file is not in the working directory: python analysis.py input.txt If input.txt is missing, the program may fail immediately. The fix is not mystical. It is usually one of the following: submit from the correct directory use a correct path copy the needed input file first Again, pwd and ls help. 4.24 Failure case study 3: request does not match workload Suppose the script requests four tasks: #SBATCH -n 4 but runs a plain serial script: python analysis.py The code itself may still run, but the request does not reflect the workload. This wastes resources and creates confusion. A good HPC user learns that job design is part of scientific and computational discipline. 4.25 Interactive jobs versus batch jobs Although this chapter emphasizes batch submission, it is useful to understand the difference conceptually. 4.25.1 Batch jobs good for repeatable workflows good for production runs good for recording output good for overnight or longer jobs 4.25.2 Interactive jobs good for exploration good for debugging good for short tests in a compute environment Beginners should learn batch first because it encourages deliberate, documented practice. 4.26 Best practices for SLURM scripts keep scripts readable name jobs clearly request realistic resources separate output and error when useful record environment details for debugging keep old scripts rather than rewriting from memory each time treat scripts as version-controlled workflow documents 4.27 Lab 1: your first real job Create job.slurm Add the minimal hello example Submit with sbatch Monitor with squeue -u $USER Inspect the output file Identify the hostname and explain why it differs from the login node 4.28 Lab 2: observe a running job Create a script with sleep 30 Submit it Monitor the job while it is running Record the job state you observe Inspect output after completion 4.29 Lab 3: separate standard output and standard error Create a script with -o and -e Print one normal message Intentionally run a bad command Inspect the .out and .err files separately Explain the difference between them 4.30 Lab 4: diagnose a broken job Break one script intentionally by doing one of the following: misspell a command refer to a missing file omit a needed module load Then diagnose using: squeue sacct output/error files pwd and ls 4.31 Stretch goal Modify a job script so that it: prints job metadata writes separate output and error files loads a required module runs a short program records start and end times Then explain each line in plain English. 4.32 Key takeaways SLURM is a scheduler and resource manager, not a direct execution command a job script is both a shell script and a resource request sbatch submits work to the queue squeue shows queued and running work sacct helps interpret finished jobs output and error files are central debugging evidence thoughtful resource requests are part of responsible HPC practice

Chapter 5 Modules, Compilers, and Your First C Program 5.1 Overview In this chapter, we move from running shell commands and submitting SLURM scripts to building executable software from source code. This matters because much HPC work depends on software that is: written by researchers customized for a lab or project compiled against particular libraries sensitive to the compiler and environment used to build it That means compilation is not an optional side topic. It is part of the practical workflow of scientific computing. By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: explain what compilation does distinguish source files from executables load a compiler module verify which compiler is active compile a small C program with gcc explain the difference between compiling and linking at a basic level interpret common build errors prepare compiled code for execution through SLURM 5.2 Mental model: source code becomes an executable A compiled workflow looks like this: source code (.c) ↓ compiler ↓ object code and linking ↓ executable binary ↓ run on LS6 At a beginner level, this picture is enough to build the right intuition: the source file is written for humans the executable is written for the machine the compiler translates the build process may involve more than one file One of the most useful early realizations is that editing source code does not automatically change the executable. If you edit the source, you must rebuild the program. 5.3 Why compilation matters on clusters Compilation matters in HPC because: software is not always preinstalled research code may need to be built from source compiler and library versions must often match toolchains affect performance and reproducibility different module environments can produce different behavior On a laptop, beginners often get used to one built-in environment. On a cluster, you should think more deliberately: What compiler am I using? What libraries is it tied to? What environment is active right now? Those are normal questions in HPC. 5.4 Modules come before compilers Before compiling, inspect the available software environment: module avail Then load a compiler environment: module load gcc module list This reinforces a key course theme: On an HPC system, software is usually exposed through the environment rather than assumed to be globally fixed forever. 5.5 Verify the compiler instead of guessing Use: which gcc gcc --version This gives you evidence for: whether gcc is available where the executable is located which version is active That matters because troubleshooting gets much easier when you know exactly which tool you are using. 5.6 Your first C program Create a file named hello.c: nano hello.c Add: #include <stdio.h> int main() { printf("Hello from C on LS6
"); return 0; } This program is intentionally tiny. The point is not complexity. The point is making the compile-and-run pipeline visible. 5.7 Compile the program gcc hello.c -o hello Interpretation: hello.c is the source file -o hello names the output executable hello 5.8 Run the executable ./hello Expected output: Hello from C on LS6 5.9 Inspect the directory Use: ls -l You should see at least: hello.c hello This is an important conceptual moment. The source file and executable are different artifacts with different purposes. 5.10 What just happened? You: wrote human-readable source code invoked a compiler produced machine-executable output ran the resulting program This is the basic compiled workflow that underlies far more complex HPC software. 5.11 Compile versus link: a useful beginner distinction You will often hear the word “compile” used to describe the entire build process. In casual conversation that is common, but it helps to know that there are two ideas present: 5.11.1 Compilation Turning source code into lower-level object code. 5.11.2 Linking Combining object code and libraries into a final executable. At first, these steps are often hidden behind a single command like: gcc hello.c -o hello But conceptually, the compiler is doing more than one job on your behalf. Why does this matter? Because some errors are compile-time errors and others are link-time errors. 5.12 Compile-time errors These happen when the compiler cannot interpret the source correctly. Example: #include <stdio.h> int main() { prinft("Hello
"); return 0; } Notice the typo: prinft instead of printf. If you compile: gcc hello.c -o hello the compiler will report a problem. A compile-time error often points to: a typo invalid syntax missing punctuation misuse of the language 5.13 Link-time errors Link-time errors happen when source files may compile individually, but the final program cannot be assembled correctly. A common beginner example involves a missing function definition. Suppose main.c calls a function add, but the file containing add is not included in the build command. Then the compiler may accept main.c, but the linker will complain that the referenced symbol cannot be resolved. This distinction helps you read build messages more intelligently: syntax problems often mean compilation trouble undefined reference problems often mean linking trouble 5.14 A simple multiple-file example Imagine the following files: 5.14.1 main.c #include <stdio.h> #include "helper.h" int main() { printf("%d
", add(2, 3)); return 0; } 5.14.2 helper.h int add(int a, int b); 5.14.3 helper.c int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } Build with: gcc main.c helper.c -o program Now the interface and implementation are both included. If you forget helper.c, you may get a link-time error. That is a valuable teaching moment. 5.15 Header files as interfaces Headers (.h) are commonly used to declare what functions are available. They do not usually hold the full implementation. At a beginner level, the main takeaway is: Headers tell the compiler what to expect. Source files provide the actual code. This makes larger projects more organized and easier to compile piece by piece. 5.16 Optimization flags A common practical compile command is: gcc hello.c -o hello -O2 Useful optimization levels to know: -O0 = no optimization -O2 = common practical default -O3 = more aggressive optimization For an introductory course, -O2 is a reasonable example because it introduces the idea that the build process can affect performance. The important conceptual point is not to memorize every flag. It is to realize that how a program is compiled can matter. 5.17 Warnings are not noise Use: gcc -Wall hello.c -o hello -Wall asks the compiler to show a wider class of warnings. Warnings do not always stop the build, but they are often useful clues. Strong beginners learn to read them early instead of ignoring them. A good principle is: If the compiler is trying to tell you something, pay attention before the project becomes larger. 5.18 A brief note on C++ A similar pattern exists for C++: g++ hello.cpp -o hello_cpp The overall workflow remains familiar: write source invoke compiler produce executable run executable This chapter uses C because it gives the cleanest first exposure to the build process. 5.19 Makefiles: why build automation exists As projects grow, repeated build commands become tiresome and error-prone. A Makefile automates the process. Create: nano Makefile Add: all: gcc -Wall -O2 hello.c -o hello Then run: make This is not just a convenience trick. It teaches a valuable lesson: Build steps are part of the workflow and should be made repeatable. 5.20 A more realistic Makefile example CC=gcc CFLAGS=-Wall -O2 all: program program: main.o helper.o $(CC) $(CFLAGS) main.o helper.o -o program main.o: main.c helper.h $(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c main.c helper.o: helper.c helper.h $(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c helper.c clean: rm -f *.o program Even if you are not yet comfortable writing Makefiles from scratch, reading one helps you understand how real projects are assembled. 5.21 Where to compile For small builds, compile on the login node. That is appropriate because compilation is often part of setup work. However, very large builds or special workflows may require different strategies depending on system policy and project needs. For this course, the safe beginner pattern is: edit on the login node compile on the login node run real jobs on compute nodes via SLURM 5.22 Running compiled code through SLURM Once the executable exists, a batch script can run it. Example: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J c_hello #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o c_hello_%j.out #SBATCH -e c_hello_%j.err ./hello Submit with: sbatch run_hello.slurm This connects Chapter 4 directly to Chapter 3: building and scheduling are different phases of the workflow. 5.23 A practical build-and-run pattern A typical beginner workflow might look like this: module load gcc gcc -Wall -O2 hello.c -o hello ls -l hello ./hello sbatch run_hello.slurm This order is instructional gold because it separates concerns clearly: load environment build executable test executable lightly run real work through the scheduler 5.24 Common compiler and build errors 5.24.1 Error type 1: syntax problem Symptoms: - missing semicolon - typo in a function name - unbalanced braces or parentheses Response: - read the line number - inspect nearby lines - fix syntax before trying other theories 5.24.2 Error type 2: missing header Symptoms: - compiler says it cannot find a header file Response: - check the include line - confirm the file exists - confirm search paths if using nonstandard locations 5.24.3 Error type 3: undefined reference Symptoms: - the compiler gets farther, then the linker complains Response: - check whether all needed .c files were included - check whether required libraries were linked 5.24.4 Error type 4: command not found Symptoms: - shell cannot find gcc Response: - check module load gcc - run which gcc 5.25 Failure case study 1: typo in source code Suppose you accidentally write: #include <stdio.h> int main() { printf("Hello
") return 0; } The missing semicolon after printf will prevent compilation. Lesson: - the executable cannot update until the source is valid 5.26 Failure case study 2: typo in the compile command Suppose you run: gcc hell.c -o hello but the file is actually named hello.c. Lesson: - sometimes the mistake is not in the source, but in the shell command itself Use: ls to confirm filenames rather than relying on memory. 5.27 Failure case study 3: object exists, executable is stale Suppose you edit hello.c, forget to rebuild, and run ./hello. You may see old behavior and think the edits failed. What really happened is simpler: source changed executable did not Lesson: - rebuilding is part of the development loop 5.28 Reading compiler messages calmly Compiler output can look intimidating because it is dense. A good beginner habit is to read it in layers: identify the first real error find the file and line number inspect the local code near that line fix one issue at a time recompile Do not scroll immediately to the bottom and panic. Often the first meaningful error is the most useful one. 5.29 Good habits for build workflows load the compiler module explicitly check which gcc and gcc --version keep source files organized rebuild after edits use -Wall automate repetitive builds with make keep run scripts separate from source files treat build commands as part of reproducible workflow documentation 5.30 Lab 1: build and run a first C program Create hello.c Load gcc Compile with gcc hello.c -o hello Run ./hello Confirm the executable exists with ls -l 5.31 Lab 2: use warnings Recompile using gcc -Wall hello.c -o hello Introduce a small issue Observe how warnings or errors change Fix the issue and recompile 5.32 Lab 3: build a multi-file program Create main.c, helper.c, and helper.h Compile them together Run the result Remove one source file from the compile command intentionally Observe and interpret the resulting error 5.33 Lab 4: automate with make Create a simple Makefile Build with make Edit the source Build again Explain why a Makefile is useful in repeatable workflows 5.34 Stretch goal Create a small program that reads input, performs a calculation, and writes output. Then: compile it with warnings enabled create a SLURM script to run it store source in src/ store the job script in jobs/ document the build and run commands in a README.md 5.35 Key takeaways source code and executables are different artifacts compiler modules are part of the HPC environment compile-time and link-time errors are different kinds of failures warnings are useful, not just noise Makefiles turn repeated build steps into reproducible workflow small programs can be built on the login node, but real execution belongs under SLURM

Chapter 6 Parallel Computing with MPI and OpenMP on LS6 6.1 Overview Up to this point, the course has focused on a basic but essential HPC workflow: connect to the system work safely on the login node manage files and environment modules submit jobs through SLURM compile programs from source The next conceptual step is parallel computing. Parallel computing is one of the main reasons HPC systems exist. A cluster is valuable not only because it is larger than a laptop, but because it allows work to be spread across multiple cores, multiple processes, and sometimes multiple nodes. This chapter introduces the two most common beginner models: MPI for distributed-memory parallelism OpenMP for shared-memory parallelism By the end of the chapter, you should be able to: explain the difference between serial and parallel execution explain the difference between processes and threads distinguish tasks, cores, and threads in a practical way describe when MPI and OpenMP are appropriate compile a small MPI program compile a small OpenMP program connect SLURM resource requests to the program model being used 6.2 Why parallel computing matters A single CPU core can execute only one stream of instructions at a time. For small tasks, that may be enough. For larger tasks, it becomes a bottleneck. Parallel computing helps when: the work can be broken into pieces those pieces can be done at the same time the time to solution matters the dataset or simulation is large many repeated calculations must be carried out efficiently In practice, HPC workloads often take one of these forms: many independent jobs one program using multiple processes one program using multiple threads hybrid workflows combining both processes and threads One of the most important learning goals here is to stop treating “parallel” as one vague idea. There are different models, and the resource request should reflect the model. 6.3 Mental model: serial versus parallel A serial program performs one step after another: Task 1 -> Task 2 -> Task 3 -> Task 4 A parallel program attempts to perform multiple pieces of work concurrently: Task 1 Task 2 -> running at the same time Task 3 Task 4 This does not automatically guarantee a speedup. Parallelism introduces overhead, coordination, and sometimes communication cost. That is why the real question is not just: Can I use more cores? It is: Does this workload benefit from more cores, and which model fits it best? 6.4 Processes, threads, tasks, and cores These terms often get blurred together. It is better to separate them. 6.4.1 Core A hardware execution unit on a CPU. 6.4.2 Process An independent running program instance with its own memory space. 6.4.3 Thread A lighter-weight execution path within a process, usually sharing memory with other threads in that same process. 6.4.4 Task (in SLURM) A scheduler concept often used to represent one process slot or one program instance in parallel execution. For beginners, the practical takeaway is: MPI usually maps naturally to multiple processes/tasks OpenMP usually maps naturally to multiple threads on one process cores are the hardware resources underneath both models 6.5 Two main models 6.6 MPI: distributed-memory parallelism MPI stands for Message Passing Interface. Mental model: you run multiple processes each process has its own memory space processes communicate by sending messages This model is especially useful when work is distributed across multiple processes and possibly multiple nodes. 6.6.1 Why MPI matters MPI is a standard approach when: the program is written for distributed memory processes must cooperate across one or more nodes the workload scales beyond the memory or cores of a single process 6.6.2 Key idea MPI processes do not automatically share memory the way threads do. Communication must be explicit. 6.7 OpenMP: shared-memory parallelism OpenMP is a threading model. Mental model: one program starts that program creates multiple threads the threads share memory inside the same node This model is especially useful when: the program is running on one node shared-memory parallelism is sufficient the work can be split across loops or sections without needing message passing 6.8 A decision rule for beginners A useful first approximation is: use MPI when the workload is designed as multiple communicating processes use OpenMP when the workload is designed as multiple threads sharing memory on a node That is not the whole world of parallel computing, but it is a strong start. 6.9 Parallelism is not the same as “asking for more resources” A repeated beginner mistake is to request more tasks or cores without changing the program. If a plain serial program is run like this: python analysis.py then asking for four tasks does not magically make it parallel. Similarly, an OpenMP program may request four cores, but if OMP_NUM_THREADS is never set, the program may not use them as intended. The scheduler grants resources. It does not rewrite your code. 6.10 MPI hello world Load an MPI environment: module load openmpi Create hello_mpi.c: #include <mpi.h> #include <stdio.h> int main(int argc, char** argv) { MPI_Init(&argc, &argv); int rank, size; MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank); MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size); printf("Hello from process %d of %d\n", rank, size); MPI_Finalize(); return 0; } Compile with: mpicc hello_mpi.c -o hello_mpi mpicc is a compiler wrapper that helps build MPI programs with the appropriate MPI libraries. 6.11 A quick local-style test A short test might use: mpirun -np 4 ./hello_mpi However, on a shared HPC system, real runs should usually go through SLURM so that resource allocation remains explicit and appropriate. 6.12 MPI through SLURM Example script: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J mpi_hello #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 4 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o mpi_%j.out #SBATCH -e mpi_%j.err module load openmpi mpirun ./hello_mpi Interpretation: -N 1 requests one node -n 4 requests four tasks the MPI program runs as four cooperating processes This is a good place to reinforce the mental model: In an MPI job, tasks usually correspond to processes. 6.13 What should the output look like? You might see something like: Hello from process 0 of 4 Hello from process 1 of 4 Hello from process 2 of 4 Hello from process 3 of 4 The order may vary because parallel processes do not necessarily print in rank order. That in itself is an important lesson: parallel execution can produce nondeterministic ordering in output. 6.14 OpenMP hello world Create hello_omp.c: #include <stdio.h> #include <omp.h> int main() { #pragma omp parallel { int id = omp_get_thread_num(); int nthreads = omp_get_num_threads(); printf("Hello from thread %d of %d\n", id, nthreads); } return 0; } Compile with: gcc -fopenmp hello_omp.c -o hello_omp The -fopenmp flag enables OpenMP support. 6.15 Run the OpenMP program Set the number of threads: export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 ./hello_omp Expected pattern: Hello from thread 0 of 4 Hello from thread 1 of 4 Hello from thread 2 of 4 Hello from thread 3 of 4 Again, output order may vary. 6.16 OpenMP through SLURM Example script: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J omp_hello #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -c 4 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o omp_%j.out #SBATCH -e omp_%j.err export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 ./hello_omp Interpretation: -n 1 requests one task/process -c 4 requests four CPU cores for that task OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 tells the program to use four threads This is the key beginner OpenMP pattern: one process, multiple threads, one shared-memory node context 6.17 Tasks versus cores: why this confuses people This is where many beginners get mixed up. 6.17.1 MPI-like pattern several tasks usually one process per task communication among processes potentially across multiple nodes 6.17.2 OpenMP-like pattern one task several cores for that task one process creates multiple threads usually within one node If you remember only one practical distinction, make it this: MPI usually scales by adding processes. OpenMP usually scales by adding threads. 6.18 A side-by-side comparison 6.18.1 MPI script pattern #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 4 module load openmpi mpirun ./hello_mpi 6.18.2 OpenMP script pattern #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -c 4 export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 ./hello_omp The resource requests are different because the program models are different. 6.19 Hybrid thinking Some applications use both MPI and OpenMP. Mental model: Multiple MPI processes ↓ Each process creates multiple threads That can be useful, but it also makes the resource request more complex. For an introductory course, it is enough to understand the concept without requiring deep hybrid optimization. 6.20 Strong scaling and weak scaling Performance discussions often use two ideas. 6.20.1 Strong scaling Same problem size, more resources. Question: - does runtime decrease when cores increase? 6.20.2 Weak scaling Problem size grows as resources grow. Question: - can the workload size increase while runtime stays roughly manageable? These ideas matter because “faster with more cores” is not the whole story. Parallel performance depends on how the workload and resources interact. 6.21 Why speedup is not automatic Beginners often imagine that doubling cores should cut time in half. Real life is more complicated. Reasons speedup may fall short: communication overhead synchronization cost load imbalance memory bottlenecks serial parts of the code poor match between program and requested resources This is why performance thinking matters as much as syntax. 6.22 A simple timing habit Use: time ./hello or time the relevant production code in a more meaningful example. The key lesson is that parallel claims should be supported by evidence, not assumption. 6.23 Common beginner mistakes 6.23.1 Mistake 1: requesting four tasks for a serial program Correction: - the code remains serial unless it is written or launched in a parallel way 6.23.2 Mistake 2: confusing tasks with threads Correction: - tasks usually correspond to processes - threads are created within a process 6.23.3 Mistake 3: using OpenMP code without setting OMP_NUM_THREADS Correction: - thread count must usually be specified deliberately 6.23.4 Mistake 4: running MPI directly on the login node as a habit Correction: - small tests may be possible, but real runs belong under SLURM 6.23.5 Mistake 5: assuming output order reveals execution order cleanly Correction: - parallel output can interleave unpredictably 6.24 Failure case study 1: resource request does not match the MPI code Suppose an MPI program is launched with only one task, but you expect four processes to appear. Possible causes: - -n was set incorrectly - the launch command did not match the intended run - the program was built or run under the wrong assumptions Lesson: - in MPI workflows, process count is a real design parameter, not a decorative number 6.25 Failure case study 2: OpenMP script requests four cores but uses one thread Suppose the job script includes: #SBATCH -c 4 but never sets: export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 The job may use only one thread. Lesson: - the scheduler grants cores - the program still needs to be told how many threads to create 6.26 Failure case study 3: parallel job is slower than the serial version This often surprises people the first time they see it. Possible reasons: - the problem is too small - communication overhead dominates - startup overhead dominates - the implementation is inefficient - the resource request does not fit the code Lesson: - parallelism is a strategy, not a magic spell 6.27 Debugging checklist for parallel jobs When a parallel run looks wrong, ask: 6.27.1 1. What model am I using? serial MPI OpenMP hybrid 6.27.2 2. Does the compile step match the model? mpicc for MPI gcc -fopenmp for OpenMP 6.27.3 3. Does the SLURM request match the model? tasks for MPI cores/threads for OpenMP 6.27.4 4. Are the relevant environment variables set? especially OMP_NUM_THREADS 6.27.5 5. What does the output file actually show? process counts thread counts unexpected ordering crashes or missing output 6.27.6 6. Is the workload large enough to reveal meaningful parallel behavior? Small toy problems often hide or distort scaling. 6.28 Best practices decide first whether the code is serial, MPI, or OpenMP request resources that match the model make process and thread counts visible in output begin with small tests measure rather than assume speedup keep job scripts readable and explicit 6.29 Lab 1: MPI hello world Create hello_mpi.c Load the MPI environment Compile with mpicc Create a SLURM script requesting four tasks Submit the job Inspect the output and explain rank and size 6.30 Lab 2: OpenMP hello world Create hello_omp.c Compile with gcc -fopenmp Create a SLURM script with one task and four cores Set OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 Submit the job Inspect the output and explain thread count 6.31 Lab 3: compare the models conceptually Create a small written comparison answering: what is a process? what is a thread? how do the SLURM requests differ for MPI and OpenMP? when would you choose each model? 6.32 Lab 4: measure a tiny scaling experiment For a simple parallel program: run with 1, 2, and 4 processes or threads record runtimes explain whether the results are convincing or limited discuss why the speedup may not be perfectly linear 6.33 Stretch goal Create a small compute kernel that performs repeated arithmetic or loop work. Implement either: an MPI version using multiple ranks, or an OpenMP version using multiple threads Then write a short reflection on whether the parallel model actually helped. 6.34 Key takeaways parallel computing is about matching workload structure to resource use MPI uses multiple processes and explicit communication OpenMP uses multiple threads in shared memory tasks, cores, and threads are related but not interchangeable terms SLURM grants resources; it does not automatically parallelize your program speedup should be measured, not assumed

Chapter 7 Containers and Reproducible HPC Workflows 7.1 Overview One of the most common frustrations in computational work is this sentence: It worked on my machine. Containers are one of the main ways modern computing tries to solve that problem. In HPC, containers help package software environments so that workflows become more portable and more reproducible. They are not magic, and they do not remove the need to understand the host system, but they are powerful when used correctly. This chapter introduces containers as a practical HPC tool. The focus is not on buzzwords. The focus is on how containers fit into real LS6-style workflow. By the end of the chapter, you should be able to: explain what a container is explain why containers matter in HPC distinguish Docker from Apptainer at a practical level pull or use a simple Apptainer image execute commands inside a container understand why directory binding matters run a containerized command through SLURM recognize common beginner container mistakes 7.2 Mental model: what a container actually is A container packages: your program required libraries supporting software dependencies environment expectations This means a container image can act like a portable execution environment. A simple mental picture is: Your code + supporting software + libraries + environment assumptions -------------------------------- Container image The key idea is not that the container replaces the whole HPC system. It does not. Instead, it packages much of the user-space environment so that software behavior becomes more consistent across contexts. 7.3 Why containers matter in HPC Containers are useful because they can reduce or manage problems like: missing dependencies conflicting library versions “works on one machine but not another” behavior hard-to-reconstruct software setups brittle custom environments In research computing, this matters because reproducibility is often not just a convenience issue. It affects whether others can repeat your workflow months later. 7.4 Containers do not eliminate the host system A very important correction: A container is not a full replacement for the HPC system around it. You still depend on: the host kernel the scheduler the filesystem cluster policies the available hardware the way input and output are connected to the environment So the right mental model is: the host system provides infrastructure the container provides a packaged software environment Both matter. 7.5 Docker versus Apptainer 7.5.1 Docker Docker is widely used in development and deployment environments. It is a major tool in software engineering and cloud workflows. However, on shared HPC systems, Docker often requires privileges or daemon-based behavior that are not a good fit for multi-user clusters. 7.5.2 Apptainer Apptainer is designed for HPC-style use. Practical advantages: better suited to shared environments commonly used without root privileges during normal execution integrates naturally into scheduler-based workflows fits better with cluster security models For this course, the practical message is simple: Docker is part of the broader container world, but Apptainer is the HPC-friendly execution model you are more likely to encounter directly on systems like LS6. 7.6 Checking for Apptainer First check whether it is already available: which apptainer If needed, load it as a module: module load apptainer Then verify again: which apptainer This mirrors earlier course logic: software availability on HPC systems is often environment-dependent. 7.7 Pulling a simple image Example: apptainer pull ubuntu.sif docker://ubuntu Interpretation: docker://ubuntu identifies the source image ubuntu.sif is the output image file .sif is a common Apptainer image format This command makes the image a tangible file in your working environment. 7.8 Run a command inside the container Use: apptainer exec ubuntu.sif echo "Hello from container" This is the cleanest way to understand container execution. You are not “booting a virtual machine.” You are asking Apptainer to run a command inside the packaged environment represented by the image. 7.9 Use an interactive shell apptainer shell ubuntu.sif This opens an interactive shell inside the container environment. Useful exploratory checks include: which python3 python3 --version ls / This helps you see that the container has its own environment logic while still running on the host. 7.10 Binding directories: the most important beginner concept One of the most common container confusions is visibility of files. A container image does not automatically expose every host path the way you might expect. Often, you need to bind directories explicitly. Example: apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif ls This makes the current working directory visible inside the container for the command being run. If your workflow depends on scripts, inputs, or outputs in the current directory, binding is often essential. 7.11 Why binding matters Without an appropriate bind, a containerized command may not see: your script your input data your working directory your intended output location That often produces a confusing symptom: The container starts fine, but it cannot find my files. Usually the container is not “broken.” The host paths simply were not connected the way you assumed. 7.12 A concrete example with a host script Suppose script.py exists in the current directory. You might try: apptainer exec ubuntu.sif python3 script.py If the container does not see that host directory, the script may not be found. A better pattern is: apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif python3 script.py This makes the relationship between host files and the container explicit. 7.13 Store images in sensible places Container images can be large. Good storage decisions matter. Practical advice: use $WORK or another project-oriented location for sizable images avoid cluttering small quota-limited areas like $HOME with large images think of images as data assets that should be placed deliberately This connects containers back to the filesystem lessons earlier in the course. 7.14 Running Python inside a container Example: apptainer exec ubuntu.sif python3 --version This is useful because it shows how containers can provide software versions that may not match the default host environment. 7.15 Containerizing a workflow through SLURM A batch script can run a containerized command just as it runs other programs. Example: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J container_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o container_%j.out #SBATCH -e container_%j.err module load apptainer apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif echo "Running inside container" Submit with: sbatch container.slurm This is an important conceptual bridge: Containers do not replace SLURM. Containers are often executed under SLURM as part of a scheduled workflow. 7.16 A more realistic containerized script run Suppose you have a Python script in the current working directory: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J container_python #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:10:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o container_py_%j.out #SBATCH -e container_py_%j.err module load apptainer apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif python3 script.py This is a realistic pattern for teaching because it combines: scheduler awareness software environment packaging explicit path handling reproducible command execution 7.17 When containers help most Containers are especially useful when: software dependencies are awkward collaborators need the same environment the host system and project software differ significantly reproducibility matters over time modules alone are not enough or become cumbersome 7.18 When modules may be enough Not every workflow needs a container. Sometimes the module system already gives you: a suitable compiler the right Python stack the right MPI stack the needed application version In those cases, using modules alone may be simpler. This is an important judgment skill: Use the tool that fits the workflow. Do not use containers just because they sound modern. 7.19 Common beginner mistakes 7.19.1 Mistake 1: assuming the container sees all host files automatically Correction: - bind the directories you actually need 7.19.2 Mistake 2: forgetting to load Apptainer Correction: - verify availability with which apptainer - load the module if needed 7.19.3 Mistake 3: storing large images in the wrong location Correction: - choose storage locations intentionally 7.19.4 Mistake 4: thinking a container replaces SLURM Correction: - scheduled execution still goes through SLURM on shared systems 7.19.5 Mistake 5: assuming every needed tool is already inside the image Correction: - inspect the image and verify commands rather than assuming 7.20 Failure case study 1: file not found inside the container Symptom: - the script exists on the host - containerized execution says the file is missing Likely cause: - the working directory was not bound into the container Fix: - add --bind $PWD or another appropriate bind path 7.21 Failure case study 2: command not found inside the image Symptom: - python3 or another expected command is missing Likely cause: - the image does not actually contain that software Fix: - inspect the image interactively - choose a more appropriate image - build or pull a container that matches the workflow 7.22 Failure case study 3: output appears in the wrong place Symptom: - the job runs, but expected output is not visible where you expect it Likely causes: - output path inside the container does not map to the host path - bind paths were incomplete or misunderstood Fix: - make host/container path relationships explicit - write outputs to bound directories intentionally 7.23 Debugging checklist for container workflows When a containerized job behaves strangely, ask: 7.23.1 1. Is Apptainer available? which apptainer 7.23.2 2. Does the image exist? ls *.sif 7.23.3 3. Can I explore it interactively? apptainer shell ubuntu.sif 7.23.4 4. Does the command I need actually exist inside the image? apptainer exec ubuntu.sif which python3 7.23.5 5. Are the needed host directories bound into the container? Check the --bind logic in the command or script. 7.23.6 6. Does the batch script still make sense as a SLURM job? Remember that the scheduler and the container are separate layers. 7.24 Best practices load Apptainer explicitly when needed inspect images rather than assuming contents bind host directories intentionally store images in appropriate storage areas keep container commands inside readable SLURM scripts document the image source and runtime assumptions in the project README 7.25 Lab 1: pull and inspect an image Load Apptainer if needed Pull a simple image Run apptainer exec with a trivial command Enter the image with apptainer shell Inspect software inside the image 7.26 Lab 2: bind the working directory Create a small host file Run a container command without a bind Observe what the container can or cannot see Repeat with --bind $PWD Explain the difference 7.27 Lab 3: run a containerized batch job Write a simple container.slurm Load Apptainer in the script Execute a containerized command Submit the job Inspect output and error files 7.28 Lab 4: compare modules and containers Write a short reflection answering: when are modules sufficient? when does a container help? what new complexity does a container introduce? 7.29 Stretch goal Run a small script through Apptainer under SLURM, with explicit bind paths and recorded output. Then write a short note explaining how another user could reproduce the workflow from scratch. 7.30 Key takeaways containers package software environments, not the entire HPC system Apptainer is the HPC-friendly container tool in this course context directory binding is one of the most important practical concepts containers often run under SLURM, not instead of SLURM modules and containers solve different workflow problems reproducibility improves when container usage is documented clearly
